summary: In this interview excerpt, scholar Rachana Kamtekar explains Plato's (and Socrates') view on moral psychology and temptation. Plato argues against the existence of 'akrasia' (weakness of will), asserting that people never knowingly act against what is best; instead, they are temporarily deceived by the immediate appearance of pleasure.

principles.statement: We never act contrary to our knowledge of what is best.
principles.rationale: When we give into temptation, we are not deliberately choosing a worse outcome over a better one. Instead, our judgment is temporarily overpowered by the false appearance that an immediate pleasure is greater or better than it actually is.
principles.source_quote: they won't act contrary to their knowledge of what is best.

mental_models.name: The Vision Analogy of Choice
mental_models.description: A lens for understanding temptation by comparing moral choice to visual perception. Just as physical proximity distorts the apparent size of an object, immediate temporal proximity distorts the apparent value of a pleasure. True knowledge acts as a 'ruler' or the 'science of optics' to correct these distorted appearances.
mental_models.example: Thinking a closer man is taller than a man further away until you measure them, just as one might judge a scoop of ice cream in front of them to be more pleasant than long-term health until they apply knowledge to override the appearance-based judgment.

anti_patterns.description: Believing in 'akratic action'—the idea that people knowingly and deliberately act against their own judgments of what is good.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It misdiagnoses human psychology. People do not choose what they know is worse; they are simply governed by 'appearance-based judgment' rather than knowledge, mistakenly perceiving the immediate temptation as better or more pleasant than it truly is.
